evidence: Compiler.exe
test.txt sample program with assignment, if/then/end, repeat/until, write, and endl
assembly strings including CheckToken, NextToken, PrintParseTree, statement, and TokenType
projects.notes: Only the packaged executable and a sample input file are present, so the description is based on observable language features and assembly symbols rather than source code.
projects.summary: A teaching-oriented compiler front end for a small imperative language, focused on token checking, parsing, and parse-tree style analysis.
projects.blog_title: Compiler for a Tiny Teaching Language
blog_paragraphs: This folder preserves a Windows desktop compiler project built around a very small teaching language. The sample input file shows a compact syntax for assignment, conditional branches, looping, and output, which makes the project feel like a classroom compiler used to explain how a language is read and validated step by step.

Even without the source, the executable metadata exposes useful clues: symbols such as CheckToken, NextToken, TokenType, and PrintParseTree suggest that the app walks through lexical analysis and syntax analysis rather than trying to be a production compiler. That makes it a strong portfolio piece for compiler construction fundamentals, especially if the website positions it as an educational parser and tokenization project.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Create a clean educational compiler thumbnail: a retro desktop coding window showing a tiny custom language with statements like assignment, if, repeat, and write; floating token cards, a branching parse tree, and subtle green terminal accents on a dark slate background. Make it feel academic, technical, and approachable.

evidence: Connect4.exe
XUI.dll
XNA assembly strings including Microsoft.Xna.Framework, Connect4Board, cubeVertices, and sceneRenderTarget
content folders for models, textures, music, bloom, fog, skybox, and UI shaders
projects.notes: No source code is present in this folder. The description is based on the packaged game build, its assets, and its XNA metadata.
projects.summary: A polished 3D Connect Four implementation built with XNA, with custom UI, music, and post-processing effects.
blog_paragraphs: This folder contains a packaged version of Connect 4 that goes well beyond a flat classroom board game. The asset structure points to a fully rendered 3D scene with board and coin models, red and yellow win states, menu UI, music, and several visual effects such as bloom, fog, and skybox rendering.

What makes the project interesting is the presentation layer. The XNA and shader-related assets suggest that the focus was not only game rules but also camera work, scene rendering, and player feedback. It reads like a student game project that tried to make a familiar game feel cinematic and game-engine driven rather than purely algorithmic.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Design a dramatic 3D Connect Four thumbnail with a glossy board floating in a lit game scene, red and yellow coins dropping into columns, a strong winner highlight, and subtle bloom and fog effects. Make it feel like an arcade-quality desktop game rather than a simple flat puzzle.

projects.summary: A runnable Windows build of a handwritten digit recognition app trained on MNIST-style data and designed for interactive classification.
blog_paragraphs: This folder packages the deployable version of a handwritten digit recognition project aimed at interactive use. The bundled training data, test data, and executable naming line up with a desktop app that can classify digits from a prepared dataset and from user-provided drawings.

As a website entry, this version is useful because it represents the user-facing side of the project rather than the development workspace. It captures the idea of shipping an educational machine learning demo as a Windows application: a compact recognizer, a simple interface, and a direct bridge between theory, dataset preparation, and immediate visual feedback.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Create a thumbnail for a handwritten digit recognition desktop app: a white drawing canvas with a bold black digit, a prediction panel showing numbers 0-9, and a subtle machine-learning vibe with grid cells and accuracy overlays. Make it feel like a practical student AI project.

evidence: C++ sound engine files using DirectSound and PlaySound
DirectX libraries dsound.lib and dxguid.lib
screenshots showing third-person combat, minimap, loot, level progression, and a skill tree
projects.notes: Only the audio subsystem source survives in this folder. The gameplay description is inferred from screenshots and the remaining sound code.
projects.summary: A third-person dungeon shooter and action-RPG prototype with combat, loot, levels, a minimap, and an upgrade tree, backed by a custom Windows audio layer.
projects.blog_title: Dungeon Shooter Prototype with RPG Progression
blog_paragraphs: This folder captures a game prototype that mixes shooter combat with light RPG progression. The screenshots show a player character navigating enclosed combat arenas, fighting enemies, collecting pickups, using a minimap, and opening a level-up screen that allocates points into movement, health, mana, attack, and multiple skills.

The surviving source focuses on sound playback, but it still tells an important story about the project. The code uses DirectSound, winmm playback, and custom event hooks for background music, hit reactions, and death sounds. Even as a partial archive, the folder presents a clear student game prototype with systems thinking behind it: combat feedback, progression loops, and presentation polish.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Generate a gritty action-game thumbnail showing a third-person hero in a dungeon arena, enemies approaching, a glowing crosshair, scattered loot, and a vertical RPG HUD with a minimap and skill icons. Use warm firelight and dark stone textures to make it feel like a student-built fantasy shooter.

projects.relationship: Packaged build variant of Graph Simulation Code.
evidence: Graph Simulation.exe
graph templates for BFS, DFS, and DJK in .cercaria files
matching assembly names and symbols with Graph Simulation Code
projects.notes: This folder is the ready-to-run build of the graph algorithm visualizer whose source exists in Graph Simulation Code.
projects.summary: A Windows build of an interactive graph algorithm teaching tool for drawing graphs and replaying BFS, DFS, and Dijkstra step by step.
blog_paragraphs: This folder holds the distributable version of a graph algorithm visualizer designed for classroom-style explanation. The packaged templates for BFS, DFS, and Dijkstra make it easy to see that the goal was not only to compute a result, but to walk a learner through the sequence of states that each algorithm produces on a graph.

As a website entry, this build is interesting because it represents the final user experience. Someone can open the app, load a prepared graph, and immediately start exploring traversal and shortest-path behavior without needing the source project. It is the kind of educational software that turns abstract algorithm lectures into something concrete and visual.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Make a bright educational thumbnail for a graph algorithm simulator: a node-edge graph on a desktop UI, colored nodes changing from white to gray to black, a shortest-path line glowing, and small labels for BFS, DFS, and Dijkstra. Keep it crisp, visual, and classroom friendly.

projects.relationship: Source project for the packaged Graph Simulation folder.
evidence: C# WinForms source code
algorithm classes for BFS, DFS, and Dijkstra
custom save and load support for .cercaria graph files
observer and state style architecture in graph, controller, and UI classes
projects.notes: This is the most complete graph project in the workspace and is the best source for any website write-up about the graph simulator.
projects.summary: A full C# WinForms project for drawing graphs, running core graph algorithms, and replaying every step in a teaching-oriented interface.
projects.blog_title: Graph Algorithm Visualizer and Teaching Tool
blog_paragraphs: Graph Simulation Code is a strong example of educational software built around algorithms instead of abstract theory alone. The application lets the user create nodes and edges, switch between directed and undirected graphs, edit properties, save and load graph templates, and then run BFS, DFS, or Dijkstra from inside a custom desktop UI.

What gives the project real depth is the way it models algorithm execution as reversible steps. Each traversal or shortest-path routine records state changes, queue operations, distances, predecessors, and pseudocode line jumps so the user can move forward and backward through the run. Combined with the graph editor, this turns the project into a practical teaching environment instead of a one-shot algorithm demo.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Create a modern algorithm-visualization thumbnail showing a graph editor window with draggable nodes, highlighted pseudocode on the side, a queue panel, and colored traversal states for BFS, DFS, and Dijkstra. Emphasize teaching, motion, and clarity.

asset names including Boy, Girl, Restaurant, Order, Client1, fast-food plate, Kidoz, and Map
projects.notes: This folder contains an installer plus a packaged game. No source code is present, so the description is inferred from build structure, executable metadata, and asset names.
projects.summary: A KidZania-themed 3D edutainment prototype centered on avatar selection, map-driven interaction, and restaurant-style gameplay.
blog_paragraphs: This folder looks like a packaged student game inspired by the KidZania brand and built as a playful 3D experience for younger audiences. The included installer, avatar textures, map assets, and skinned-model support all point to a complete desktop game flow rather than a rough technical prototype.

The content names suggest a restaurant or service-job scenario: boys and girls as selectable characters, clients, orders, plates, and a restaurant scene. That gives the project an edutainment feel, where the game is not only about movement in a 3D space but also about role play, task completion, and simple interactions that fit the KidZania theme.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Design a colorful KidZania-style thumbnail with a cheerful 3D restaurant scene, a boy and girl avatar, floating order icons, bright signage, and a playful city-map vibe. Keep it family friendly, energetic, and clearly game-like.

projects.relationship: Source project for the packaged digits hand written recognition folder.
evidence: C# WinForms source code
MNIST train and test files
classification, training, confusion matrix, and drawing UI code
image resize and binary threshold preprocessing
projects.notes: The method names say KNN, but the implementation behaves more like a nearest-centroid classifier built from digit class averages and binary thresholding.
projects.summary: A C# desktop app for handwritten digit recognition that trains on MNIST-style data, visualizes digit templates, and lets users draw or load images for classification.
blog_paragraphs: Pattern Project is an educational machine learning application built around handwritten digit recognition. It reads MNIST-style training and test files, computes class representatives, visualizes digit patterns, and provides a simple interface for exploring both the training output and the final predictions.

The most engaging part of the project is the interaction loop. A user can draw a digit directly on the canvas or open an image file, the app resizes it to the expected 28 by 28 format, converts it into a binary representation, and then runs a distance-based classification step. The project also reports overall accuracy and confusion matrix data, which gives it a strong data-science and evaluation angle rather than making it a pure demo.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Create an AI-themed thumbnail for a handwritten digit recognizer: a central hand-drawn digit on a white canvas, side panels with 0-9 templates, a confusion matrix heatmap, and subtle grid overlays suggesting MNIST preprocessing. Keep it clean, academic, and visual.

evidence: Final Graduation Project.exe
Emgu CV, OpenCV, GPU, OCR, and OpenTK libraries
training data with repeated Human and Car labels
assembly strings including CameraCapture, ProcessImage, FaceSegmentation, ClassifyObjects, ConvertGroundToGraph, and MovingObject
projects.notes: This looks more like a rule-based or trained scene-understanding pipeline than a modern end-to-end captioning model. The provided training data suggests a compact object vocabulary rather than open-domain narration.
projects.summary: A graduation project focused on scene understanding: capture an image, segment and classify objects, model spatial structure, and turn the result into a narrated interpretation.
projects.blog_title: Scene Narrating System for Visual Understanding
blog_paragraphs: Scene Narrating System appears to be a final-year computer vision project aimed at translating visual scenes into structured interpretations that can be narrated or explained. The executable is bundled with a full Emgu CV and OpenCV stack, which suggests a traditional image-processing pipeline rather than a black-box end-to-end model.

The available clues point to a sequence of operations: load or capture an image, convert it through grayscale and binary stages, segment contours, classify detected objects, and transform the ground layout into a graph-like representation. The included training data strongly suggests at least human and car categories, so this project is best described as an early scene-understanding and object-based narration system built with classic computer vision tools.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Generate a computer-vision thumbnail showing a real-world street or room image being transformed into overlays: detected humans and cars, contour boxes, graph nodes connecting scene elements, and a narration panel on the side. Make it look like a serious graduation project in visual AI.

evidence: Security.exe, Client.exe, Server.exe, and SecurityLib.dll
assembly strings including Cryptography, Encryptor, Decryptor, Monoalphabetic, Polyalphabetic, KeyExchange, MultiplicativeInverse, EncryptionRounds, and DecryptionRounds
projects.notes: Only packaged binaries are present, but the names and form resources are detailed enough to identify the project as a teaching-oriented cryptography suite.
projects.summary: A Windows security toolkit that explores classical encryption methods and basic client-server cryptography workflows through interactive desktop tools.
projects.blog_title: Classical Cryptography Toolkit with Client-Server Demos
blog_paragraphs: Security Project looks like a desktop suite built to explain cryptography by making it interactive. The executable names and symbols point to encryption and decryption workflows, classical cipher families, arithmetic helpers, and a small networked setup with client and server components.

The strongest website angle here is education. Terms such as monoalphabetic, polyalphabetic, key exchange, and multiplicative inverse suggest that the project was designed to help users see how classical cryptographic ideas work in practice instead of treating security as a purely theoretical topic. It reads like a student-friendly sandbox for experimenting with cipher mechanics and message exchange.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Create a cybersecurity teaching-project thumbnail with cipher wheels, lock icons, encrypted text streams, and two desktop panels labeled client and server exchanging keys. Use sharp blue and amber highlights and keep the feel academic rather than corporate.

evidence: C++ source code for Student, Course, Department, General, and Interface modules
console forms and menu flow in interface.cpp and interface.h
file I/O, maps, sorting, and grade management logic
projects.notes: Includes.h references Vector.h and Vector.cpp, but those files are not present in the folder. The main project logic is still clear from the existing source files.
projects.summary: A feature-rich C++ console system for managing students, departments, courses, prerequisites, grades, and ranking reports.
projects.blog_title: Student Course Management System in C++
blog_paragraphs: This project is a full academic management system implemented as a C++ console application. It models students, departments, and courses as separate entities, then ties them together through enrollment, prerequisites, grades, and department-level reporting. The interface code suggests a large menu-driven experience that supports add, search, update, display, and delete flows.

What stands out is the breadth of the operations. The system can assign courses to students, calculate total marks, report grades in detail, display course prerequisites, and sort students by performance across courses and departments. It is more than a CRUD exercise; it is a small information system that tries to reflect how academic data behaves across multiple related structures.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Design a structured software-project thumbnail for a student course management system: dashboard-like cards for students, departments, and courses, grade tables, prerequisite arrows, and a clean console-to-system visual style. Use academic colors and make the project feel organized and data driven.

evidence: Unity project files and builds
ProjectVersion.txt showing Unity 2018.2.4f1
custom scripts for Darius movement, attacks, spells, cooldown timers, enemies, scoring, and inventory
Unity post-processing and NavMesh references
projects.notes: The project combines original Darius-themed mechanics with remnants of Unity's Survival Shooter tutorial structure, which makes the customization work easy to identify.
projects.summary: A Unity survival game that reworks the classic Survival Shooter format into a Darius-themed arena combat experience with melee combos, spells, cooldowns, and enemy waves.
projects.blog_title: Survival Darius: Unity Survival Combat Rework
blog_paragraphs: Survival Darius is the most game-engine heavy project in the workspace. Built in Unity 2018, it adapts the familiar survival-arena loop into a character-driven combat game centered on Darius-style attacks and abilities. The player can move through the arena, chain melee attacks, trigger spells on number keys, manage cooldowns, and survive against spawning enemies while the score system tracks progress.

The source shows clear customization on top of the base survival structure: spell-specific particle systems, cooldown UI, pull and explosion effects, custom animation triggers, inventory slots, and a themed asset library for characters, effects, and icons. This makes the project a good showcase of taking a tutorial foundation and turning it into something with a distinct identity and gameplay flavor.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Create a high-energy Unity game thumbnail for Survival Darius: a battle-worn hero at the center of a zombie-filled arena, swirling spell effects, cooldown icons, a score HUD, and dramatic cinematic lighting. Make it feel like a custom action-survival game with MOBA-inspired abilities.

projects.relationship: Source project for the packaged Turing Machine Simulator folder.
evidence: C# WinForms source code
graph-based state editor with start and final states
single-tape Turing machine execution logic
step-by-step teaching mode with next and previous controls and tape visualization
projects.notes: Despite the broad folder name, the implemented project is specifically a visual single-tape Turing machine builder and simulator.
projects.summary: A graphical Turing machine editor and simulator that lets users build states and transitions, enter tape input, and replay execution step by step.
projects.blog_title: Visual Turing Machine Builder for Theory of Computation
blog_paragraphs: This project turns theory of computation into something visual and interactive. Instead of presenting Turing machines as static diagrams in lecture notes, the application lets the user draw states and transitions, mark start and final states, attach transition rules, enter tape input, and then simulate the machine directly inside a desktop interface.

The strongest part of the project is the teaching mode. Execution is broken into explicit steps, the active state changes color, the tape is updated as symbols are written, and the user can move forward and backward through the simulation. That makes the software valuable as both a study aid and a demonstration tool for explaining acceptance, rejection, and state transitions in a much more intuitive way.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Design a bold educational thumbnail for a Turing machine simulator: a state graph with labeled transitions on the left, a tape with a moving head on the right, and a highlighted accept state glowing green. Make it feel visual, technical, and immediately understandable.

projects.notes: This folder is the standalone runnable build of the Turing machine simulator whose source lives in Theory Of Computation.
projects.summary: A distributable Windows build of the visual Turing machine simulator, focused on interactive state editing and tape-based execution replay.
blog_paragraphs: This folder packages the finished Windows version of the Turing machine simulator. It preserves the interface assets, the executable, and the polished UI layer needed to explore machine design and tape execution without opening the source project.

For a website portfolio, this build version is useful because it highlights the final user-facing experience. The project is not just about implementing theory; it is about turning theoretical state machines into something a learner can draw, run, and inspect visually step by step.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Create a polished software thumbnail for a Turing machine simulator build: a desktop app window showing a tape, a highlighted current state, arrows between states, and a big Accepted or Rejected indicator. Keep the composition clear and academic.

evidence: C++ source code for game loop, board drawing, player naming, turns, and win checking
ASCII-art console rendering
Windows console color handling
projects.notes: This is a simple but complete two-player console game and is one of the clearest beginner-friendly projects in the workspace.
projects.summary: A C++ console implementation of tic-tac-toe with colored ASCII rendering, player names, and a full turn-based loop.
blog_paragraphs: XOCode is a compact console game that implements the full tic-tac-toe loop in C++. It asks for player names, prints a position guide, draws a stylized ASCII board, alternates turns between X and O, and checks for all horizontal, vertical, and diagonal win conditions.

What gives the project charm is its presentation. Instead of treating the console as plain text, it uses Windows console colors and block characters to create a more playful interface. That makes it a good early game-programming project: small enough to understand quickly, but complete enough to show real control flow, state tracking, and user interaction.
projects.thumbnail_prompt: Create a retro coding-game thumbnail for a console tic-tac-toe project: a glowing terminal grid with X and O marks, green and amber text, blocky ASCII styling, and a subtle C++ code backdrop. Make it nostalgic and immediately readable.
